title: Courgette bread
subtitle: Moist, slightly sweet quick loaf using grated courgette to add moisture and a green tint.
excerpt: Courgette bread works on the same principle as carrot cake: a finely grated vegetable releases water as it bakes, keeping the crumb moist for days. The courgette flavour is barely detectable in the finished loaf; it acts primarily as a moisture carrier. One loaf, one bowl, about an hour in the oven.
sourceNotes: Zucchini bread (courgette bread) became popular in the United States in the 1960s and 1970s as a way to use glut summer courgettes from the kitchen garden. The technique follows the standard American quick-bread method: oil rather than butter for a moister crumb, and fine grating to distribute the vegetable evenly through the batter. This version uses British courgettes in season, whole milk instead of oil for a lighter result, and reduces the sweetness compared to American recipes. Temperatures are for a fan oven.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice when cool and freeze flat, separated by baking paper. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: This loaf is moister on day two and three than on the day it is baked. Store wrapped at room temperature for up to four days.

recipeTools.notes: Standard 2 lb (900 g) loaf tin, greased and lined.
Use the large-hole side for grating courgette.

content.text: Courgettes are about 95 percent water. When you grate them finely and fold the shreds into a batter, that water releases slowly during baking, keeping the crumb moist for three or four days. The taste is subtle enough that most people would not identify it as courgette unless told. What they notice is that the loaf stays soft longer than a plain banana or carrot version.
The key is not to squeeze the grated courgette dry. That moisture is the whole point. Grate it directly into the batter bowl and fold everything together.

items.prepNote: medium, about 350 g total, coarsely grated

content.text: Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin with baking paper.
Grate {{courgette}} on the large holes of a box grater directly into a large bowl. Do not squeeze out the liquid.
Add {{sunflower-oil}}, {{eggs}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{vanilla-extract}} to the bowl with the courgette. Stir until combined.
Add {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Fold together with a spatula until no dry flour remains. Fold in {{walnuts}} if using.

Scrape into the prepared tin. Bake for 60 to 70 minutes. Test with a skewer in the centre: it should come out with moist crumbs but no wet batter. The top will be a rich brown and slightly domed. Cover loosely with foil after 45 minutes if it is browning too fast.
Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Leave for at least 30 minutes before slicing.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Courgette bread is very forgiving, but the vegetable's moisture content can vary.

items.symptom: Loaf still wet in the centre after 70 minutes.
items.cause: Courgettes were particularly wet, or oven temperature too low.
items.fix: Cover with foil and continue baking in 10-minute intervals. Very large courgettes have more water; medium ones around 150 to 175 g each are ideal.

items.symptom: Loaf collapsed slightly in the centre after cooling.
items.cause: Underbaked, or removed from the tin too soon.
items.fix: The skewer test must read truly clean, not just less wet. Leave in the tin for the full 10 minutes before turning out.
